EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet streak passing south through Florida propelled a cold front into the Florida peninsula, resulting in several hours of freezing temperatures over the Nature Coast.  Isolated spots in central Florida had an hour or two of freezing temperatures.  No significant crop damage was reported.||Interior Levy county had 3 to 4 hours of freezing temperatures.   A few stations had an hour or two of hard freezing conditions.  The coldest temperature reported was 27 degrees at the Chiefland COOP site.||Interior Citrus county had 3 to 4 hours of freezing temperatures, with isolated cold spots recording up to 7 hours.  A few stations had an hour or two of hard freezing conditions.  The coldest temperature reported was 27 degrees at the Crystal River AWOS (CGC).||Sumter county had 2 to 3 hours of freezing temperatures with isolated cold spots reporting up to 7.5 hours of freezing temperatures.  Isolated spots reported an hour or two of hard freezing conditions.  The coldest temperature reported was 28 degrees at the Okahumpka FAWN site near the eastern Sumter county border.||Interior Hernando county had around 3 hours of freezing temperatures.  The coldest temperature reported was 30 degrees at the Brooksville ASOS.||Interior Pasco county had around 3 hours of freezing temperatures.  The coldest temperature reported was 30 degrees at the Zephyrhills AWOS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interior Levy county had 3 to 4 hours of freezing temperatures.   A few stations had an hour or two of hard freezing conditions.  The coldest temperature reported was 27 degrees at the Chiefland COOP site.

Significant straight-line wind damage occurred along two long swaths, from southwest Lake County to Cocoa, and from Orlando to Cape Canaveral. Winds were measured up to 86 mph at two observing sites in Orange County. Much the surrounding areas of Lake, Orange, and Brevard Counties (as well as adjacent portions of Seminole and Osceola Counties) experienced minor wind damage. A woman was injured in Cocoa when a tree fell on the Florida room that she was entering from outdoors. Damage estimates totalled over $3M, of which $2M was from the collapse of a storage warehouse under construction in Orlando. The remainder of the cost estimate was a result of falling trees and mainly minor structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter reported nickel size hail at County Road 405 near Walmart in Titusville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper level disturbance rotating around a mean longwave trough west of the area brought copious amounts of moisture into the area.  The KTAE rawinsonde sounding on the evening of February 22nd measured a PWAT value of 1.77 inches, which was the 2nd highest PWAT observed during the month of February at that location.  Rainfall amounts generally between 9 and 14 inches were observed during the 5 day period between Feb 22-26 across the Tallahassee area with similar amounts across the remainder of the Florida Panhandle.  This led to areas of flooding.  There were also some severe storms during the early morning hours of Feb 26th.  The southeast big bend saw significantly less rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were blown down across the county, especially around Crawfordville.  Around 90% of the Crawfordville substation lost power.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of severe convection developed across interior central Florida as the west coast sea breeze pushed inland from the Gulf of Mexico. Discrete cells within the line became severe, with several becoming supercells as they moved east toward the coast. Two different supercells each produced a short-track EF0 tornado in Brevard County, while hail up to the size of quarters fell in Altamonte Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter observed quarter sized hail in Altamonte Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist air mass provided ample fuel for a prefrontal trough that developed ahead of a strong cold front that moved through Florida on the 24th.  The prefrontal trough produced a line of showers and thunderstorms, with a few storms becoming severe with damaging downburst winds and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A SKYWARN spotter reported quarter sized hail in Hudson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into South Florida and stalled resulting in isolated strong to severe thunderstorms over metropolitan Broward and Palm Beach Counties during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Store Manager of Lowe's Home Improvement Center witnessed a tornado in the storm parking lot. Trees were downed, cars moved and a stop sign was broken. No other damage or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions led to a brush fire in Ruskin that grew to 50 acres and damaged property, and shutting down a stretch of Interstate 75 due to smoke.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brush fire developed in Ruskin and eventually spread to 50 acres.  The fire was reported by print media to have damaged a mobile home, several carnival food trucks, a warehouse, and a few old racing cars.  Additionally, smoke from the fire shut down Interstate 75 between mile markers 229 and 240 for several hours.

Two different supercells each produced a short-track EF0 tornado in Brevard County, while hail up to the size of quarters fell in Altamonte Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado with winds estimated between 70 and 80 mph touched down near the Intersection of U.S. 1 and Dixon Boulevard in Cocoa and travelled northeast to the Indian River where it became a waterspout. The tornado produced minor damage to the roofs and outbuilding of several businesses along U.S. 1, with power lines also downed. Numerous trees were downed along Dixon Boulevard and Indian River Drive. DI27; DOD2; EXP, DI8; DOD2; EXP.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado with winds estimated at 75 to 85 mph, affected primarily the Charolais Estates and Colfax Landing subdivisions in Viera/Rockledge. The damage damage was embedded within a larger swath of strong straight-line winds which affected areas extending farther to the west and east. Several homes experienced damage to roof tiles and soffits. Concrete roof tiles were carried downstream and penetrated several windows. Other metal debris was carried downstream and a fence was blown down. Numerous pool screen enclosures totally collapsed. DI2; DOD1; UB.

No crop damage was reported.||In Levy county, freezing temperatures were recorded at inland locations for 2-4 hours. The coldest temperature reported was 29 degrees at the COOP station 6 miles southeast of Chiefland.||In Citrus county, freezing temperatures were recorded at inland locations for up to 4 hours. The coldest temperature reported was 32 degrees at the AWOS site at Crystal River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Levy county, freezing temperatures were recorded at inland locations for 2-4 hours. The coldest temperature reported was 29 degrees at the COOP station 6 miles southeast of Chiefland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry and breezy conditions fueled wildfire growth in Flagler and Marion counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1145 am EST, Marion County Fire/Rescue reported a wildfire that burned 5 acres was out of control about 3 miles east of Wiersdale. At  2 pm, Marion County Fire/Rescue reported a new wildfire near Fisher Lanes Place in Ocklawaha which grew quickly to 50 acres. At 730 pm, a newspaper reported that the Hopkins Prairie Wildfire about 6 miles NE of Juniper Springs consumed around 600 acres with 24 structures burned. The cost of damage was unknown. A shelter was opened at the Forest Assembly of God along State Road 40, and 4 people stayed one night. On March 3, the Park Service reported that the fire burned 10 homes and had burned a total of 1900 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system moved from the northern Brevard coastal waters northward and produced gale conditions along the Volusia County coastline. Wind gusts of 45 to 54 mph were common for several hours. While there was scattered convection in the area, the strongest wind gusts were from gradient, non-convective winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather Underground mesonet station WU51 near Ormond Beach measured non-convective gusts of 43 knots. Some minor powerline damage occurred in the area due to falling tree branches.

A few stations had an hour or two of hard freezing conditions.  The coldest temperature reported was 27 degrees at the Chiefland COOP site.||Interior Citrus county had 3 to 4 hours of freezing temperatures, with isolated cold spots recording up to 7 hours.  A few stations had an hour or two of hard freezing conditions.  The coldest temperature reported was 27 degrees at the Crystal River AWOS (CGC).||Sumter county had 2 to 3 hours of freezing temperatures with isolated cold spots reporting up to 7.5 hours of freezing temperatures.  Isolated spots reported an hour or two of hard freezing conditions.  The coldest temperature reported was 28 degrees at the Okahumpka FAWN site near the eastern Sumter county border.||Interior Hernando county had around 3 hours of freezing temperatures.  The coldest temperature reported was 30 degrees at the Brooksville ASOS.||Interior Pasco county had around 3 hours of freezing temperatures.  The coldest temperature reported was 30 degrees at the Zephyrhills AWOS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interior Pasco county had around 3 hours of freezing temperatures.  The coldest temperature reported was 30 degrees at the Zephyrhills AWOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm became severe and produced wind gusts up to 60 mph in Hobe Sound, within southeast Martin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty Orlando TV meteorologist reported damage to a tennis court in the tailwind airpark subdivision. Estimated wind gusts at the time were 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm became severe and produced wind gusts up to 60 mph in Hobe Sound, within southeast Martin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter in Hobe Sound estimated wind gusts of 60 mph and small hail as a severe thunderstorm moved east across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 25 year old male was transported to the hospital and later died after being pulled from the water during the afternoon hours behind the Legacy by the Sea Resort, located near the 15000 block of Front Beach Road.  Strong rip currents were in the area with single red flags flying.

Gusty winds behind a cold front resulted in a couple of trees being blown down in northeastern Jackson county near Hornsville.  A peak gust of 37 mph occurred at the Marianna airport around the time of the report.

A deepening low pressure system moved from the northern Brevard coastal waters northward and produced gale conditions along the Volusia County coastline. Wind gusts of 45 to 54 mph were common for several hours. While there was scattered convection in the area, the strongest wind gusts were from gradient, non-convective winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather Underground mesonet station WU51 measured non-convective wind gusts of 46 knots. Some minor powerline damage occurred in the area due to falling tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper level disturbance rotating around a mean longwave trough west of the area brought copious amounts of moisture into the area.  The KTAE rawinsonde sounding on the evening of February 22nd measured a PWAT value of 1.77 inches, which was the 2nd highest PWAT observed during the month of February at that location.  Rainfall amounts generally between 9 and 14 inches were observed during the 5 day period between Feb 22-26 across the Tallahassee area with similar amounts across the remainder of the Florida Panhandle.  This led to areas of flooding.  There were also some severe storms during the early morning hours of Feb 26th.  The southeast big bend saw significantly less rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chris Smith of WJHG posted a picture on Facebook showing water affecting homes along the 1700 block of New Hampshire Avenue in Lynn Haven due to heavy rainfall.  Water was surrounding the homes and the driveways were not visible.  Rainfall rates of 2 to 3 inches per hour were in the area, and the ground was saturated from previous heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of severe convection developed across interior central Florida as the west coast sea breeze pushed inland from the Gulf of Mexico. Discrete cells within the line became severe, with several becoming supercells as they moved east toward the coast. Two different supercells each produced a short-track EF0 tornado in Brevard County, while hail up to the size of quarters fell in Altamonte Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter observed penny sized hail in Apopka.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper level disturbance rotating around a mean longwave trough west of the area brought copious amounts of moisture into the area.  The KTAE rawinsonde sounding on the evening of February 22nd measured a PWAT value of 1.77 inches, which was the 2nd highest PWAT observed during the month of February at that location.  Rainfall amounts generally between 9 and 14 inches were observed during the 5 day period between Feb 22-26 across the Tallahassee area with similar amounts across the remainder of the Florida Panhandle.  This led to areas of flooding.  There were also some severe storms during the early morning hours of Feb 26th.  The southeast big bend saw significantly less rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant areal flooding occurred across Calhoun county due to prolonged periods of moderate to heavy rainfall.  Four day rainfall totals of around a foot were estimated across the county between Feb 22-26.  The emergency manager estimated over $1 million in damages across the county, mainly due to road repair.

The KTAE rawinsonde sounding on the evening of February 22nd measured a PWAT value of 1.77 inches, which was the 2nd highest PWAT observed during the month of February at that location.  Rainfall amounts generally between 9 and 14 inches were observed during the 5 day period between Feb 22-26 across the Tallahassee area with similar amounts across the remainder of the Florida Panhandle.  This led to areas of flooding.  There were also some severe storms during the early morning hours of Feb 26th.  The southeast big bend saw significantly less rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant areal flooding occurred across Jackson county due to prolonged periods of moderate to heavy rainfall. Four day rainfall totals of 8-12 inches were estimated across the county between Feb 22-26. The emergency manager estimated over $1.8 million in damages across the county, mainly due to road repair.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper level trough of low pressure over the eastern U.S. produced severe thunderstorms over Miami-Dade County during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail was reported by a trained spotter in South Miami near the intersection of SW 63rd Terrace and SW 66th Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper level trough of low pressure over the eastern U.S. produced severe thunderstorms over Miami-Dade County during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail was reported by an off duty NWS employee at the intersection of NE 69th Street and Biscayne Blvd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure area over the northeast Gulf of Mexico along with a strong upper level trough of low pressure across the Florida peninsula combined to develop severe thunderstorms over much of southeast Florida during the afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported nickel sized hail at the intersection of Hillsboro Blvd. and U.S. 441.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure area over the northeast Gulf of Mexico along with a strong upper level trough of low pressure across the Florida peninsula combined to develop severe thunderstorms over much of southeast Florida during the afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet reported 58 mph wind gust at Palmview Elementary School courtesy of WeatherBug.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough crossing Florida interacted with an already moist atmosphere to enhance sea breeze thunderstorms throughout the afternoon. Some of these storms produced hail up to golf ball sized, and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire department reported a large tree was knocked down onto a house near Tighe Avenue and Lennar Avenue in Seffner.  The damage was widely reported by the media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough crossing Florida interacted with an already moist atmosphere to enhance sea breeze thunderstorms throughout the afternoon. Some of these storms produced hail up to golf ball sized, and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Call Center reported three trees and a large flagpole down in Arcadia.  One tree was at SE Pointer Avenue south of Arcadia Municipal Airport, another tree was on SW Baldwin Street, and the third tree was on Parkview Road.  The flagpole was knocked down on the corner of Polk Avenue and Magnolia Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper level trough covered the eastern U.S. with abundant tropical moisture across South Florida resulting in scattered thunderstorms along the southeast coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout moved onshore and was caught on video with minor roof damage and broken windows reported in the vicinity of La Coquille Villas along with downed trees on both sides of Highway A1A.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across South Florida during the afternoon causing scattered showers and a few thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee reported dime-sized hail in Coconut Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across South Florida during the afternoon causing scattered showers and a few thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported nickel-sized hail on Key Biscayne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous strong to severe thunderstorms developed within a very unstable airmass, aided by an upper level disturbance. Several of the storms produced penny to quarter sized hail and damaging winds across Lake, Orange and Seminole Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS Storm Survey revealed an approximate three-quarter mile wide swath of straight-line thunderstorm wind damage extending 1.25 miles from Trimble Park to Lake Ola and Orange Blossom Trail in Tangerine. The damage consisted of mainly large trees toppled (most with extensive canopies), large tree branches downed and some fence damage. Very minor structural damage was noted to a few homes (chimney damage to two story homes). Maximum winds were estimated to be 70-80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper level trough covered the eastern U.S. with abundant tropical moisture across South Florida resulting in scattered thunderstorms along the southeast coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary thunderstorm over Coral Gables produced torrential rainfall over a period of about two hours with rainfall estimates in excess of six inches and an unofficial, measured report just west of Coral Gables of 7.56 inches. Law enforcement reported water was entering garages along Anderson Rd. between Palermo Ave. and Camilo Ave. Water was also reported to be entering businesses along Miracle Mile near Galiano St. A parking lot was flooded along Biltmore Way with water reported to be up to the bottom of car doors.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure over the eastern U.S. caused an east wind along the Florida east coast of 15 to 20 mph. This led to the formation of dangerous rip currents at the beaches along the northern coast of Palm Beach County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several swimmers had to be rescued during the afternoon and early evening at the beaches along the coast of northern Palm Beach County, some nearly drowning. ||The first report was just after 2 pm EDT at Ocean Cay Park in Jupiter where two people nearly drowned. They were pulled from the water and both were taken to Jupiter Medical Center where one was reported in critical condition. Around 445 PM, six other swimmers were also rescued from the dangerous rips in the same area with one was taken to Jupiter Medical Center. ||Around 6 pm EDT, two male swimmers struggled in the waves at Lake Worth Pier but managed to reach shore and declined further treatment. ||The last reported rescues were shortly after 7 pm EDT when eight other swimmers were caught in rip currents in two separate incidents off the Lake Worth Pier. Six of the swimmers nearly drowned and were taken to Bethesda Memorial Hospital in Boynton Beach and JFK Medical Center in Atlantis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure area over the northeast Gulf of Mexico along with a strong upper level trough of low pressure across the Florida peninsula combined to develop severe thunderstorms over much of southeast Florida during the afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet reported 58 mph sustained wind with a 75 mph gust at North Broward Medical Center via WeatherBug. The instrument is located on top of a four-story building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure area over the northeast Gulf of Mexico along with a strong upper level trough of low pressure across the Florida peninsula combined to develop severe thunderstorms over much of southeast Florida during the afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pompano Beach ASOS reported a thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph located at the Pompano Beach Airpark.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across the western Florida panhandle during the early evening hours producing sporadic damage.

A weak upper level trough of low pressure moved across South Florida along with a stationary frontal boundary over North Florida resulting in isolated severe thunderstorms over the Miami metropolitan region during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet located 3 miles north northwest of Naranja reported a thunderstorm wind gust of 61 mph. Broadcast media also relayed a report of a carport getting blown onto a residence in the same area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across South Florida causing scattered thunderstorms to develop ahead of the front during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 72 mph was measured at the Seminole Tribe Headquarters in Hollywood courtesy of WeatherBug.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the southeast states produced an east wind along the Florida Atlantic coast of 10 to 15 mph and moderate rip currents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Palm Beach Post reported a woman was pulled from the surf at the Lake Worth Beach due to a possible rip current. The woman did not require CPR but was taken to the JFK Medical Center in Atlantis for follow-up treatment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front over central Florida lifted north on the afternoon of the 14th, with an area of showers and thunderstorm developing over the coastal waters and moving into the Florida Peninsula.  One of these storms produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pasco county emergency management reported wind gusts estimated at 40 to 50 MPH uprooted several trees and power lines near the intersection of 16th Street and North Avenue in Zephyrhills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure area over the northeast Gulf of Mexico along with a strong upper level trough of low pressure across the Florida peninsula combined to develop severe thunderstorms over much of southeast Florida during the afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported quarter sized hail along Alligator Alley...I75... near Mile Marker 35.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure area over the northeast Gulf of Mexico along with a strong upper level trough of low pressure across the Florida peninsula combined to develop severe thunderstorms over much of southeast Florida during the afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sawgrass Springs Middle School Weather Bug reported a thunderstorm wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure area over the northeast Gulf of Mexico along with a strong upper level trough of low pressure across the Florida peninsula combined to develop severe thunderstorms over much of southeast Florida during the afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Florida Power and Light reported to the Palm Beach Post that a tree fell on powerlines in North Palm Beach...near 1660 12 Oaks Way. It was also reported that there was tree damage at a condominium development in the same area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure area over the northeast Gulf of Mexico along with a strong upper level trough of low pressure across the Florida peninsula combined to develop severe thunderstorms over much of southeast Florida during the afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported quarter sized hail 1 mile northwest of Tequesta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weak surface low pressure systems draped across the the deep south including the central Florida peninsula combined with an approaching cold front over a very saturated airmass triggered a cluster of storms over Nassau county that produced a strong outflow that tracked SE over western Duval county. When this outflowed encountered ESE flow and feeder bands moving inland from the Atlantic, the cell became nearly stationary over southern Duval and NE Clay counties where it produced flash flooding during the evening. The cell pulsed up and became severe over northern St. Johns county where low level helicity was greatly increased from the strong 20 kt surface winds from the ESE on the north side of a meso low that lifted northward over Alachua and Putnam counties. The cell began to strong very strong and deep layer rotation over northern St. Johns county, and a tornado warning was issued. The cell then collapsed within the next few radar scans, but weak rotation was still present. When a very strong downburst raced southward over St. Johns county, the downburst produced widespread damage to farmland near Elkton and a weak EF1 tornado touched down, on the bookend of the downburst. Flooding rainfall and deep hail drifts of penny size hail caused extensive damage to cropland near Elkton, with severe financial impact to farmers in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of water occurred over the roads 2-3 feet deep in some areas with some structural flooding. A lot of flooding occurred between Bambi Lane and 105th Street and Interstate 295 and Blanding Blvd. The public also reported vehicles were stranded between Overland Park Blvd and Bear Creek Road. Clay County Emergency Management reported significant street flooding with impassable roads and stranded vehicles between Kingsley Avenue and Wells Road and Blanding Blvd and US-17. The Sheriff's Office reported that the Julington Creek Bridge was closed due to high water on both ends of the bridge. The bridge was later opened. The cost of damage was estimated and is likely grossly underestimated. A storm spotter measured 4.32 inches of rainfall between 6 pm and 8 pm in the Jacksonville westside. Street flooding was reported at Harlow Blvd and Cinderella Road, Harlow Blvd between Boy Blue Road and Bambi Lane, and Boy Blue Road was completely under water from Harlow Blvd to Anvers Blvd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure area over the northeast Gulf of Mexico along with a strong upper level trough of low pressure across the Florida peninsula combined to develop severe thunderstorms over much of southeast Florida during the afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported quarter to half dollar sized hail near the Florida Turnpike and the Sawgrass Expressway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across South Florida causing scattered thunderstorms to develop ahead of the front during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A skywarn spotter reported a 65 mph thunderstorm wind gust near the intersection of SW 55th Ave. and SW 19th St in Davie. The spotter also reported a funnel cloud but then lost visual contact with it. Street flooding was also reported along Peters Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several strong to severe thunderstorms developed as they interacted with the sea breeze near the east coast, from Brevard County to Martin County. Wind gusts up to 62 mph and hail to quarter size were observed. Some minor wind damage occurred in Rockledge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail was reported in Rockledge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several strong to severe thunderstorms developed as they interacted with the sea breeze near the east coast, from Brevard County to Martin County. Wind gusts up to 62 mph and hail to quarter size were observed. Some minor wind damage occurred in Rockledge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: USAF wind tower 9404 in Rockledge recorded a peak wind gust of 62 mph as a severe thunderstorm moved across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several strong to severe thunderstorms developed as they interacted with the sea breeze near the east coast, from Brevard County to Martin County. Wind gusts up to 62 mph and hail to quarter size were observed. Some minor wind damage occurred in Rockledge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brevard Emergency Management reported minor wind damage to two mobile homes in Rockledge. Carports, awnings and a porch were damaged by the thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several strong to severe thunderstorms developed as they interacted with the sea breeze near the east coast, from Brevard County to Martin County. Wind gusts up to 62 mph and hail to quarter size were observed. Some minor wind damage occurred in Rockledge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A twitter report indicated quarter-sized hail in Merritt Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several strong to severe thunderstorms developed as they interacted with the sea breeze near the east coast, from Brevard County to Martin County. Wind gusts up to 62 mph and hail to quarter size were observed. Some minor wind damage occurred in Rockledge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter in Stuart observed hail falling for several minutes, increasing from nickel to quarter size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep layer cyclonic flow and very high moisture content produced several days of soaking rainfall during a develop local Nor'Easter event. After locally heavy rainfall on May 2 and 3, strong onshore flow and gusty winds of 40 to 50 mph started to caused trees to come down over inland northeast Florida from Bradford county to Flagler county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total rainfall total over the past 24 hours was 7.19 inches. Four inches were measured since midnight on the 3rd. Additional rainfall amounts from May 2 through 9:30 am on May 3: 7.75 inches in Bulow, 7 inches in Central Palm Coast; 5.75 inches in south central Palm Coast. Widespread power outages and sporadic trees down occurred across the county over the 2 day period due to saturated grounds and gusty onshore flow with non-convective wind gusts up to 40 to 50 mph on May 3rd. On May 3rd, the county Park Department closed Princess Place Preserve as a precaution due to coastal flooding and the chance of trees falling due to saturated grounds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several strong to severe thunderstorms developed as they interacted with the sea breeze near the east coast, from Brevard County to Martin County. Wind gusts up to 62 mph and hail to quarter size were observed. Some minor wind damage occurred in Rockledge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter observed nickel sized hail in Stuart.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several strong to severe thunderstorms developed as they interacted with the sea breeze near the east coast, from Brevard County to Martin County. Wind gusts up to 62 mph and hail to quarter size were observed. Some minor wind damage occurred in Rockledge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter observed nickel sized hail at the Treasure Coast Mall on US Highway 1 in Jensen Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud formed over the Atlantic, then moved overhead of the barrier island at Indian Harbour Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports indicate that a funnel cloud formed over the Atlantic, then moved over the barrier island at Indian Harbour Beach. The observers reported that the funnel cloud dissipated and reformed three times over a 10-minute period, but did not touch down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms formed over the Treasure Coast and moved to the northwest through the area.  As it interacted with the east coast sea breeze, one shower developed a brief funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer at Saint Lucie County Aiport (KFPR) reported a funnel cloud 3 miles northeast of the airfield. The funnel lasted for about 1 minute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Andrea approached the Florida Big Bend from the southwest, producing heavy rain across east-central Florida over a 3-day period.  Area-wide totals of 2-4 inches were common, with isolated, maximum totals of 5-6 inches within Orange, Volusia and Osceola Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Florida Water Management District station S61W (SWOF1) recorded 5.08 of rain during Tropical Storm Andrea.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Andrea approached the Florida Big Bend from the southwest, producing heavy rain across east-central Florida over a 3-day period.  Area-wide totals of 2-4 inches were common, with isolated, maximum totals of 5-6 inches within Orange, Volusia and Osceola Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS station FL-OR-1 recorded a storm total rainfall of 5.29 during Tropical Storm Andrea.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level trough of low pressure moved across South Florida producing scattered strong to severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported penny-sized hail at North Perry Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level trough of low pressure moved across South Florida producing scattered strong to severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported quarter-sized hail 2 miles north-northeast of Medley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Port St. Lucie police officer was injured when lightning struck only 10 to 15 feet away from where he was standing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Port St. Lucie police officer was injured after lightning struck less than 15 feet away when he was investigating a domestic call at the Saints Golf Course, in the 1500 block of Westmoreland Boulevard. The officer couldn't hear for 45 minutes after the strike and said he could feel the charge leaving his body through his fingertips. The glue that attached the sole and body of his boot melted into his sock. He was held overnight at a hospital, but was released the following day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Westerly flow assisted the west coast seabreeze in pushing through east-central Florida during the afternoon hours. Storms sparked along the seabreeze boundary and intensified as they interacted with the east coast seabreeze, which was pinned along the east coast. As they intensified, the storms produced severe wind gusts and a funnel cloud in Brevard County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tractor trailer traveling northbound on I-95 and its load was lifted off its base by a thunderstorm wind gust, causing the trailer to overturn onto the highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered afternoon thunderstorms during typical summer afternoons on June 24th and 25th.  Lightning struck one home in Jackson county and hail was reported in Bay county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning caused a house fire at 2878 Magnolia Blossom Lane in the Indian Springs subdivision east of Marianna.  The Jackson County Floridian newspaper article stated that damages to the house and its contents totaled around $600,000.  The time of the strike was estimated by lightning data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and storms formed during the afternoon and produced outflow boundaries, which collided, causing additional showers and storms. The steering flow was very weak, resulting in a very slow storm motion. The heavy rain produced temporary, mostly minor flooding, particularly in Lake County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused loose sand in a road construction project area in Mount Dora to shift and partially bury four cars, also blocking a hotel entryway. The event was reported by the Orlando Sentinel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in cyclic fashion over Key West and adjacent nearshore waters over a duration of three hours, producing widespread street flooding and flooding to businesses along the lower elevations of Key West.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding up to two feet in depth was observed at the corner of White and Eaton Streets in Key West.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Westerly flow assisted the west coast seabreeze in pushing through east-central Florida during the afternoon hours. Storms sparked along the seabreeze boundary and intensified as they interacted with the east coast seabreeze, which was pinned along the east coast. As they intensified, the storms produced severe wind gusts and a funnel cloud in Brevard County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted over the intracoastal waters near the Melbourne Causeway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With low pressure developing in the eastern Gulf of Mexico, East Central Florida was under the influence of very moist southeasterly flow, which combined with diurnal heating and seabreeze forcing to produce afternoon thunderstorms.  One of the thunderstorms produced strong winds which caused damage in Apopka in Orange County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust tore off a portion of the canvas roof of the Apopka Amphitheater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex surface low pressure system extending from offshore the Mid Atlantic coast to central Florida, supported by a sharp upper trough of low pressure extending southward into the Gulf of Mexico, produced strong thunderstorms in the Florida Straits, which moved northeast across portions of the Florida Keys during the early morning hours of April 5, 2013.  One thunderstorm produced a waterspout which moved ashore the extreme southern portion of Big Pine Key.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout was observed by the public, backlit by frequent lightning strikes approaching long beach estates from the west-southwest, passing to the south of the Newfound Harbor Keys.  The waterspout moved ashore along the oceanside of Long Beach Drive and passed over the public observer's property, a bed-and-breakfast inn.  It was at this landfall location that the tornado was at its maximum intensity and width of about 80 yards.  Widespread damage to large limbs of native hardwood trees occurred, and one royal palm snapped at a level above 15 feet.  A ground deck with four kayaks became airborne with the dock dropped within mangroves beyond 30 yards from the original location.  A large unmounted above-ground cistern containing nearly 200 gallons of water slide just over 15 yards, knocking a large camping trailer off its front mount.  Bolts securing a large grill to wood ground decking were sheared off.  While numerous tables and lounge chairs were carried to the leeward side of the property, one 40-lb steel lounge chair was lifted to the edge of the roof line of the two-story residence, crushing the rain gutter, before settling in the lee of the structure.  Small gravel and pea rock along the waterfront caused moderate to severe paint chipping on the oceanside of several homes.  Screens including their wood frames were torn off several residences' oceanside porches, with one home losing about 40 percent of its oceanside vinyl siding.  A phone line was downed along Long Beach Drive.  ||The tornado continued across mud flats along the southeast edge of Coupon Bight, where only isolated damage to mangrove branches was observed.  Small limbs were observed downed across Long Beach Drive between Southeast Point and U.S. Highway 1, eventually crossed through a campground just south of the Spanish Harbor Channel Bridge.  Several tents including one large aluminum-framed dome tent strucutre were overturned before the tornado likely passed into Spanish Harbor Channel.   The intensity of the tornado at Spanish Harbor Channel had decreased since its original landfall with estimated winds near 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Westerly flow assisted the west coast seabreeze in pushing through east-central Florida during the afternoon hours. Storms sparked along the seabreeze boundary and intensified as they interacted with the east coast seabreeze, which was pinned along the east coast. As they intensified, the storms produced severe wind gusts and a funnel cloud in Brevard County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: U.S. Air Force wind tower 1007 measured a wind gust of 55 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed within southerly flow and produced damaging wind gusts in Fruitland Park which downed numerous trees. Several sections of a fence were also bent over.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter reported at least seven trees blown down when a thunderstorm moved through Fruitland Park.  Some of the trees were large oaks with diamters of 16-18 inches. Nearby, several sections of fence were bent over by the wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Andrea crossed the forecast area from the Gulf of Mexico. SE Ga and NE Fl were on the northeast side of the storm...which focused onshore convergent bands. A few tornadoes were spawned along the Duval and Nassau county Atlantic coasts...with gusty tropical storm force winds and locally heavy rainfall over inland areas. Twenty-four hour rainfall amounts ranged from 1 to 4 inches...with the highest value of 3.62 inches 4 ESE of Waycross. Rainfall amounts decreased to the SE with an observation site 5 NNW of Spuds only reporting 0.06 inches. Wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph were common along the coast and estuary systems. The highest gust over northeast Fl was 38 knots measured at Naval Air Station Jacksonville from the south on the 6th at 11:06 pm. The highest gust over southeast Georgia was 39 knots measured from the WNW at 6:28 am at the St. Simons Airport. The highest wind gust along the coast was reported from a WeatherFlow (mesonet) station located at the Jacksonville Beach Pier with a speed of 72 knots reported at 4:37 pm on the 6th. This gust was likely caused by a passing waterspout that moved onshore near Mayport. The lowest pressure measured was from the Jekyll Island WeatherFlow (mesonet) station of 993.0 mb on the 7th at 12:30 am. The highest wind gust measured over the adjacent Atlantic waters was 39 kts at buoy 41012 43 NM east of St. Augustine on the 6th at 10:50 pm. The C-Man station SAUF1 in St. Augustine measured a gust to 42 knots on the 6th at 7:58 pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado tracked along County Road 137 and damaged trees and power lines in its path. There was metal roofing and siding tin blow into tree tops along the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Westerly flow assisted the west coast seabreeze in pushing through east-central Florida during the afternoon hours. Storms sparked along the seabreeze boundary and intensified as they interacted with the east coast seabreeze, which was pinned along the east coast. As they intensified, the storms produced severe wind gusts and a funnel cloud in Brevard County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: U.S. Air Force wind tower 1204 measured a 53 knot wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system affected the area at the end of January with unusually strong wind fields.  The 500 mb winds were as high as 110 knots with the upper level trough, and 850 mb winds reached around 70 knots in the local area. These very strong low level winds combined with SBCAPE values of 300-500 j/kg produced widespread straight line wind damage across the area with numerous trees and power lines blown down as a squall line moved through the area.  Strong non-convective related winds also occurred ahead of the main line of convection and resulted in a few trees being blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large limbs were down on power lines across the county causing power outages.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The east coast sea breeze moved inland during the afternoon across Miami-Dade County producing a line of cumulus clouds and large funnel clouds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of a large funnel cloud were received in the South Miami-Dade County area from areas as far away as Kendall to Cutler Bay. An off duty NWS employee reported that the funnel cloud was located a few miles south-southwest of Zoo Miami near the intersection of SW 190th St. and SW 137th Ave. The funnel lasted about 12 minutes. There were no reports of touchdown. The event was covered quite extensively by broadcast and print media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The east coast sea breeze moved inland during the afternoon across Miami-Dade County producing a line of cumulus clouds and large funnel clouds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty NWS employee, along with a Channel 4 helicopter and several reports from the public, indicated a funnel cloud near the Dolphin Mall in Sweetwater. The funnel lasted a few minutes and then dissipated.

A strong cold front moved into the Nature Coast on the morning of the 26th, and passed south through Tampa bay through the afternoon.  A squall line moved out ahead of the cold front, producing damaging downburst winds and a waterspout in the Tampa Bay that twice moved onshore as a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cedar Key fire chief reported widespread wind damage with numerous trees and power lines down as well as damage to docks and boats in several locations around Cedar Key.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NDBC C-MAN site at Cedar Key, CRDF1, measured a 79 knot thunderstorm wind gust.

The tornado moved into the Channelside District where is damaged trees, signs, awnings, windows, tables, and chairs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed over the Florida peninsula on February 16th with cool air filling in behind the front.  Temperatures fell down to near freezing along the Nature Coast on the morning of the 17th, but the coldest temperatures were recorded on the morning of the 18th.||Temperatures stayed below freezing for up to 8 hours over the Nature Coast with hard freeze conditions occurring for up to 6 hours.  Over Central Florida, freezing temperatures were recorded for around 2 to 4 hours with isolated locations recording a few hours of hard freeze conditions.  Parts of southwest Florida experienced 2 to 3 hours of sub-freezing temperatures. ||No significant crop damage was reported.||Levy County recorded sub-freezing temperatures for around 6 to 8 hours across interior portions of the county.  The coldest temperature reported was 25 degrees at the RAWS site at Lower Suwannee.||Citrus County recorded sub-freezing temperatures for around 6 to 8 hours across interior portions of the county.  The coldest temperature reported was 27 degrees at the AWOS in Inverness.||Hernando County recorded sub-freezing temperatures for around 8 hours over interior portions of the county.  The coldest temperature reported was 27 degrees at the mesonet site 2 miles northwest of Weeki Wachee.||Sumter County recorded sub-freezing temperatures for 4 to 6 hours across much of the county.  The coldest temperature reported was 25 degrees at the FAWN site at Okahumpka, near the eastern Sumter county border.||Pasco County recorded sub-freezing temperatures for around 6 hours across much of the interior portions of the county.  The coldest temperature reported was 27 degrees at the mesonet site 1 mile east-northeast of Zepherhills.||Isolated spots in interior Hillsborough County recorded freezing temperatures for around 2 hours.  The coldest temperature reported was 32 degrees at the AWOS at Tampa Executive Airport.||Isolated spots in Polk County recorded sub-freezing temperatures for around 2 hours hours.  The coldest temperature reported was 30 degrees at the AWOS in Bartow.||Isolated spots in interior Manatee County recorded sub-freezing temperatures for up to 2 hours.  The coldest temperature reported was 30 degrees at the mesonet site 4 miles west-northwest of Lake Manatee State Park.| |Isolated spots in interior Sarasota County recorded sub-freezing temperatures for around 3 hours.  The coldest temperature reported was 30 degrees at the FAWN site at North Port.||Hardee County recorded sub-freezing temperatures for up to 4 hours across much of the county.  The coldest temperature reported was 28 degrees at the COOP site at Wauchula.||Isolated spots in Highlands County recorded sub-freezing temperatures for up to 4 hours.  The coldest temperature reported was 28 degrees at the COOP site at Archbold.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Levy County recorded sub-freezing temperatures for around 6 to 8 hours across interior portions of the county.  The coldest temperature reported was 25 degrees at the RAWS site at Lower Suwannee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sea breeze boundary collisions took place along the I-75 corridor. Easterly flow pushed the storms into the Tampa metro area producing strong winds and damage to trees, powerlines and area homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a report of down burst winds that knocked over power poles and trees, and damaged a car port and balcony near Gulf Winds Circle and Gulf View Drive in Hernando Beach. The time was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Easterly flow produced numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across the area. The storms produced sub severe hail and downed numerous trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local television station relayed a report of a downed tree on Hillsborough Avenue in Seffner. The time was estimated from Doppler radar signatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Easterly flow produced numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across the area. The storms produced sub severe hail and downed numerous trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local television station relayed a report via social media which showed a picture of a downed tree in the Meadowbrook Village neighborhood in Town N' Country.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Easterly flow produced numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across the area. The storms produced sub severe hail and downed numerous trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local television station reported dime-size hail at their station in Westchase.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Easterly wind flow across the peninsula produces another active afternoon of severe weather across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike hit the North Greenwood Recreation and Aquatic Center on north Martin Luther King Jr. Road causing a fire in the facility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Easterly wind flow across the peninsula produces another active afternoon of severe weather across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station 3 miles SW of Eagle Lake in Polk county recorded a wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Easterly wind flow across the peninsula produces another active afternoon of severe weather across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A TV station relayed a report of downed trees and damage to billboards near the intersection of Fort Harrison Ave and Belleair Rd in Clearwater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall causing numerous flood impacts in Pasco and Hernando Counties. Also, a sub-severe thunderstorm produced a lightning strike causing a brief fire at a local elementary school.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pasco County Emergency Management relayed a report of five cars stranded on a flood prone road near San Antonio. Doppler Radar estimated 4 inches of rain had fallen at the time of the report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall causing numerous flood impacts in Pasco and Hernando Counties. Also, a sub-severe thunderstorm produced a lightning strike causing a brief fire at a local elementary school.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pasco County Emergency Management reported heavy rainfall causing flooding on the roads near the Dade City Hospital. Doppler radar estimated up to 3 inches of rain had fallen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall causing numerous flood impacts in Pasco and Hernando Counties. Also, a sub-severe thunderstorm produced a lightning strike causing a brief fire at a local elementary school.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike hit Mintz Elementary School which caused a brief fire which was quickly put out. No estimate on damage costs to the school was available.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sea breeze boundaries collided over Southwest Florida to produce sea breeze thunderstorms.  One of these storms developed a quick down burst wind gust in Lee county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Regional Southwest International Airport in Fort Myers measured a 50 knot (58 MPH) thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong storms moved through Jackson county, downing one tree.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was blown down in Alford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 26 year old male was swept away and drowned after saving two girls and his best friend from rip currents.

The Panama City News Herald reported a rip current fatality occurred at Panama City Beach around 7 pm June 8th as well as three rescues that required hospitalization around 5 pm June 9th.  Two of the three rescued were located near Edgewater Beach Resort, and the third was located near Continental Condominiums.  The rescued included a 17 year old male, 22 year old female, and a female in her 70s.  A moderate risk of rip currents was forecast.||In addition, The Northwest Florida Daily News Newspaper reported that two more men drowned while swimming off Santa Rosa Beach, Fla., near Scenic Highway 30-A in Walton County on June 9th. The newspaper reported that the incidents happened on an unguarded section of beach behind South Spooky Lane. Witnesses said the first victim, 44-year-old Annazar Nazarov of Memphis, was 75 yards off shore and the second victim, 48-year-old William Haithcox of Spartanburg, S.C., went into the water to attempt a rescue. Lifeguards responded from the nearest tower, about a half mile away, and pulled the men ashore, but were not in time.  A high risk of rip currents was forecast. ||On June 10th around 10 am, the Panama City News Herald reported that deputies responded to a swimmer in distress call at about the 23011 Front Beach Road area on Panama City Beach.  One deputy along with the distressed swimmer were hospitalized.  A moderate risk of rip currents was forecast at the time of the rescue.||On the afternoon of June 11th, WMBB-TV reported that two distressed swimmers required hospitalization from behind the Days Inn and behind the Sunbird.  In addition, the Panama City News Herald reported that a 67 year old man drowned off of Panama City Beach during the afternoon.  A moderate risk of rip currents was forecast, and single red flag were flying.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man died trying to rescue a 10 year old boy from a rip current at Hanna Park. The boy was pulled to safety, but the 62 year old died a short time later at the hospital. The event was logged as an indirect fatality.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A male was killed in the surf zone. The cause was unknown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The media reported a drowning about 1 mile or less from the St. Mary River inlet. The cause of drowning death was either due to tidal currents or rip currents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Easterly flow allowed for strong thunderstorms to form late in the evening on the 16th. During the early morning hours on the 17th, these storms moved closer to the coast again producing tree damage and one thunderstorm sparked a house fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused a house fire to a two-story house in the East Lake Neighborhood. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike downed a couple of power lines in Tallahassee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a couple of power lines down due to a lightning strike near 1111 High Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal cluster of showers with isolated thunderstorms tracked northeast across north Florida during the afternoon and evening. ||A progressive upper level trough over the central U.S. moved east as the lead short wave trough lifted northeast across the Ohio Valley during the day.  Well ahead of a cold front, Northeast Florida and Southeast Ga were located in the warm sector of this system with a strongly sheared atmosphere. The 12z and 00z JAX Upper Air Sounding showed 0-3KM helicity values between 300-400 m2/s2. Surface based instability was weak but enough for thunderstorm activity over mainly northeast Florida. Two thunderstorms merged together as thunderstorms tracked from the nature coast across the Florida peninsula over Flagler County. The role of the storms interaction and merger most likely assisted in the subsequent storm enhancement and tornado genesis across Palm Coast, Florida. The storm strengthened with 40 knots of rotational velocity around 5.7 kft over Palm Coast in Flagler county, and a tornado warning was issued at 6:54 pm EST. The first confirmed tornado touchdown damage was recieved at 7 pm that nigth in Palm Coast. There were no injuries nor fatalities reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downburst with maximum wind speeds of 100 mph caused significant damage. A 18x18 wooden barn building was damaged and moved off of its concrete foundation with the metal roof of the building thrown into a nearby tree. The time of the event was based on radar imagery. Penny size hail was also reported. The cost of damage was estimated for inclusion in Storm Data and is likely an underestimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low deepened and tracked northeastward through the Florida Panhandle and southern Georgia during the morning hours. Ahead of this low, a warm front was able to move northward and bring an unstable airmass into portions of the Florida Panhandle. Strong shear in place aided in the development of isolated severe thunderstorms with a couple of weak tornadoes and straight line wind damage reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado was rated as an EF1 with max winds estimated around 95 mph and a path length of 2.92 miles.  Damage consisted primarily of trees snapped and uprooted with power lines down, but there was also some structural damage to outbuildings and homes.  The tornado tracked through the Land's Landing area and through Cochran Landing and out at Cox Landing.  The worst damage was on Cox Landing Road where one home and vehicle suffered significant damage.  The homeowner's boat was also blown away by the tornado and was missing.  The homeowner was not home at the time.  At least a few people witnessed this tornado, and dual-pol radar showed a well defined tornado debris signature (TDS) with this storm extending up to around 10,000 ft.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow led to scattered thunderstorms moving through much of West Central and Southwest Florida.  A lightning strike from one of these storms sparked a house fire in Hillsborough county causing minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported an attic fire started by lightning on Lido Drive in Brandon.  Fire crews were able to put the fire out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sea breeze boundary collisions took place along the I-75 corridor. Easterly flow pushed the storms into the Tampa metro area producing strong winds and damage to trees, powerlines and area homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highlands County Emergency Management reported roof damage to 6 homes in Lake Josephine. One home on Leona Dr. lost the majority of the roof.  Trees, power lines, and a few carports were blown down as well. The time was estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sea breeze boundary collisions took place along the I-75 corridor. Easterly flow pushed the storms into the Tampa metro area producing strong winds and damage to trees, powerlines and area homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused roof damage to a lanai in the Town N Country area. The time was estimated by radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reports numerous 3 to 4 inch diameter tree branches down on Benjamin Road just north of Sligh Avenue.  A power pole and wires were leaning into a tree on the side of the road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: State emergency management relayed a report of a house struck by lightning with minor damage.  No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal cluster of showers with isolated thunderstorms tracked northeast across north Florida during the afternoon and evening. ||A progressive upper level trough over the central U.S. moved east as the lead short wave trough lifted northeast across the Ohio Valley during the day.  Well ahead of a cold front, Northeast Florida and Southeast Ga were located in the warm sector of this system with a strongly sheared atmosphere. The 12z and 00z JAX Upper Air Sounding showed 0-3KM helicity values between 300-400 m2/s2. Surface based instability was weak but enough for thunderstorm activity over mainly northeast Florida. Two thunderstorms merged together as thunderstorms tracked from the nature coast across the Florida peninsula over Flagler County. The role of the storms interaction and merger most likely assisted in the subsequent storm enhancement and tornado genesis across Palm Coast, Florida. The storm strengthened with 40 knots of rotational velocity around 5.7 kft over Palm Coast in Flagler county, and a tornado warning was issued at 6:54 pm EST. The first confirmed tornado touchdown damage was recieved at 7 pm that nigth in Palm Coast. There were no injuries nor fatalities reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the evening hours, between 655 pm and 710 pm EST on December 14, 2013, a tornado with maximum strength of EF1 on the Enhanced Fujita Scale touched down and crossed the northern sections of Palm Coast in Flagler County Florida. The tornado initially touched down north of Espanola on an intermittend track. The tornado then intensified to its maximum strength of 95 to 110 across the B section (Indian Trails and Pine Lakes neighborhoods) and western F sections of Palm Coast on a continuous path ranging in width from approximately 75 yards to a maximum of around 150 yards on Bannbury Lane. The tornado weakened as it moved northeast toward the coast with a path width of 25 to 50 yards across the eastern F section and Hammock area.  ||The location of the initial touchdown was near 29.52 degrees north latitude and 81.31 degrees west longitude. The location of the final damage was near 29.61 degrees north latitude and 81.19 degrees west longitude.  The total distance between the initial touchdown and final damage was approximately 9.5 miles with the longest continuous path approximately 1 mile in the B section of Palm Coast.  ||Media reported 7 homes were destroyed and more than 150 damaged when the tornado struck Palm Coast. Palm Coast city officials stated that 142 homes were partially damaged in the Indian Trails neighborhood and 22 homes had moderate damage. The damage was estimated at more than $5 million. No injuries were reported. The area was cluttered with downed trees and house debris.  The tornado downed power lines, blew out windows, knocked down trees and power poles and ripped away roof shingles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the Florida Peninsula early on the morning of the 27th, with cold air moving in through the rest of the day and that evening.  The coldest temperatures were recorded on the morning of the 28th (Thanksgiving Day) with freezing temperatures observed in Levy and Citrus counties. A few isolated spots south of that briefly recorded freezing temperatures for less than 2 hours. The coldest temperature reported was 28 degrees at the mesonet site 4E Chassahowitzka (DW7434). No crop damage was reported.||Levy County recorded sub-freezing temperatures for around 4 to 5 hours across much of the county.  The coldest temperature reported was 29 degrees at both the FAWN station at Bronson, and at the mesonet station 2W Inglis (YNKTW).||Citrus County recorded sub-freezing temperatures for around 2 to 3 hours across much of the county.  The coldest temperature reported was 28 degrees at the mesonet site 4E Chassahowitzka (DW7434).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Levy County recorded sub-freezing temperatures for around 4 to 5 hours across much of the county.  The coldest temperature reported was 29 degrees at both the FAWN station at Bronson, and at the mesonet station 2 miles west of Inglis (YNKTW).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong local Nor'Easter conditions persisted for a couple of days which brought minor coastal flooding and beach erosion around the times of high tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NOS tidal station at Fernandina Beach measured an elevated water level of 8.59 ft relative to MLLW which was 0.75 ft above the predicted level. Minor coastal flooding at this location typically begins at 8.5 ft relative to MLLW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent onshore winds of 10 to 15 knots helped create favorable conditions for rip currents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Clearwater woman drowned in the Gulf of Mexico on her birthday five blocks north of the Clearwater Pier. Her husband reported deep water and strong currents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure near the Yucatan Peninsula provided a deep flow of tropical air into South Florida resulting in scattered very heavy showers and a few thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent heavy rains from slow moving showers and thunderstorms produced an isolated area of flash flooding near the Falls Shopping Mall in Miami-Dade County during the late afternoon and early evening. Measured rainfall amounts were in the range of 7 to 10 inches in the matter of just a few hours. The first report of flooding was received at 5:10 PM EDT with streets nearly impassable and the Falls parking lot almost completely under water. Several cars were also reported to have been flooded. Water entered structures in the Village at the Falls Condo development with the Oak Ridge Residential Community also reporting water intrusion into a vehicle which caused a total loss of the car. Residents moved their cars to side streets to avoid further damage. Some rainfall reports received included a local Ham Operator with 7.71 inches in his rain gauge near the intersection of Sunset Dr. and 97th Ave. with a mesonet site near 63rd Ave. and SW 120th St. recording 9.05 inches. The heaviest total was received from a Cocorahs observer in Kendall with 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours, a couple of which were severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down at Baker Creek Road near Jenny Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours, a couple of which were severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down near Blue Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across southern Georgia and moved into northern Florida, producing occasional instances of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large oak tree was blown down on Route 59 and Moccasin Gap Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A collision of three boundaries including the east coast sea breeze moving inland, and two outflow boundaries, one from the south and one from the west, converge under and intensifying thunderstorm over the Fort Caroline area. Rapid convergence and uplift spawned a brief tornado which caused structural damage to an apartment complex and widespread tree and some roof damage to homes in the area. One injury occurred. The slow storm motion also produced significant rainfall amounts, with caused flooding from Arlington toward the Mayport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The sea breezes and several outflow boundaries converged over east Jacksonville Thursday afternoon around 4 pm...then the multicell cluster that developed drifted slowly eastward offshore of the Atlantic coast through 5:30 pm. Strong winds were reported with these storms, with the Naval Air Station in Jacksonville reporting a 41mph wind gust at 3:52pm). WFO Jacksonville conducted a storm survey Thursday evening and confirmed an upper end EF-1 tornado touched down in the Arlington area, ENE of downtown Jacksonville around 4:20 pm EDT along Monument Road and Derringer Road. The tornado briefly reached EF-1 intensity just south of Willowood Drive where it damaged an apartment complex. The Jacksonville Sheriff's office reported 5-6 homes were damaged and 15-20 structures were damaged. There was 1 injury associated with wind damage about 1 mile SW of the Fort Caroline area. The cost of damage was estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down on Centerville Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture and an upper level trough over the Florida Peninsula caused scattered afternoon thunderstorms.  One of these storms produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports were received of several trees knocked down and minor damage to a couple docks along Lakeview Drive. Time was estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture allowed for sea breeze thunderstorms to develop in the afternoon.  Some of these storms produced heavy rain and damaging lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a report of street flooding up to the bumpers of cars in Clearwater near East Bay Drive and U.S. 19.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture allowed for sea breeze thunderstorms to develop in the afternoon.  Some of these storms produced heavy rain and damaging lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Florida State Watch Office reported a house was damaged by lightning in Largo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few isolated showers developed over Tampa Bay. One of which produced a brief waterspout that moved onshore at MacDill Air Force Base.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The weather observer at MacDill Air Force Base reported waterspout over Tampa Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across southern Georgia and moved into northern Florida, producing occasional instances of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down on Killarney Way.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary, ahead of a line of thunderstorms, moved off the coast at Cocoa Beach. A brief gustando developed along the boundary as it crossed the coast near the Coconuts on the Beach restaurant. Several people on the beach were hit by debris, resulting in injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gustnado formed along an outflow boundary ahead of a weakening line of thunderstorms approaching the coast. The gustnado developed on Cocoa Beach, adjacent to the Coconuts on the Beach restaurant. The circulation lifted dozens of beach umbrellas and other objects at least 100 feet into the sky. Although the event only lasted about 30 seconds, the beach was highly populated and debris struck several people, resulting in injuries. Although most injuries were minor, a 15-year old boy suffered a head injury after being struck by a flying canopy, and was transported by ambulance to an area hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several citizens viewed a funnel cloud at the Kennedy Space Center and a weather spotter observed another funnel cloud in Port St. Lucie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports were received of a funnel cloud over the Kennedy Space Center. An isolated light shower was occurring in the immediate vicinity of the funnel cloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak easterly winds and deep moisture allowed for sea breeze thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon. Some of these storms produced damaging lightning strikes and wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Florida State Watch Office relayed a report of a house on Alcazar Way in South St. Petersburg that was struck by lightning, causing a fire. Broadcast media reported that the fire only damage one bedroom. Time was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several citizens viewed a funnel cloud at the Kennedy Space Center and a weather spotter observed another funnel cloud in Port St. Lucie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter reported a funnel cloud visible just north of Prima Vista Boulevard in Port St. Lucie. The duration of the funnel was only one minute and its formation occurred near an isolated thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sea breeze collision over the Florida Gulf Coast allowed for scattered afternoon thunderstorms along the west half of the Florida Peninsula.  Some of these storms produced large hail and damaging lightning strikes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail of up to the size of quarters was reported on Lake Thomas Road in Land O' Lakes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sea breeze collision over the Florida Gulf Coast allowed for scattered afternoon thunderstorms along the west half of the Florida Peninsula.  Some of these storms produced large hail and damaging lightning strikes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service Forecast Office in Ruskin, FL was struck by lightning causing widespread damage to electronics across the office.  Several computer motherboards, monitors, security systems, a HAM radio, and internet routers were all damaged.  Time was estimated from the point when several computer systems failed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent strong storms produced heavy rainfall amounts that resulted in flash flooding across portions of Escambia, Santa Rosa and Okaloosa counties during the afternoon and early evening hours on July 5th.  A few thunderstorms became severe as they moved north through the Destin area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Santa Rosa Boulevard at Starfish Avenue and Seahorse Avenue on Okaloosa Island was nearly completely submerged with water at least 6 inches in depth.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief tornado moved north from Choctawhatchee Bay and quickly dissipated after producing minor damage to the roofing of some buildings.  A few roofing tiles were blown off.
