EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with isolated occurrences of strong downburst winds and localized flooding developed during the mid to late afternoon hours on July 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines blown down on Lloyd Street and Scenic Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with isolated occurrences of strong downburst winds and localized flooding developed during the mid to late afternoon hours on July 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Waterspout came ashore as a tornado near Oak Drive and quickly dissipated.  A few trees were blown over onto road by tornado and a few power lines were left sagging.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with isolated occurrences of strong downburst winds and localized flooding developed during the mid to late afternoon hours on July 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automobiles were stalled due to flood waters on U.S. Highway 98 where water was at least one foot deep in places.  Water was also reported at least 6 inches over the roadway on Mercado Street and Avenida Del Sol in Navarre.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with isolated occurrences of strong downburst winds and localized flooding developed during the mid to late afternoon hours on July 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood water at least 2 foot deep reported in a house on Aurora Drive in Holley By The Sea Community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with isolated occurrences of strong downburst winds and localized flooding developed during the mid to late afternoon hours on July 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood water at least 2 foot deep reported in a house on Aurora Drive in Holley By The Sea Community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist air mass south of a stalled frontal boundary across the northern Gulf of Mexico provided instability for afternoon thunderstorms along the Gulf Coast of Florida. Many of these storms produced damaging wind gusts.  Also, one storm caused lightning damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highlands County 911 Call Center reported railroad crossing gates knocked down onto a road due to strong thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist air mass south of a stalled frontal boundary across the northern Gulf of Mexico provided instability for afternoon thunderstorms along the Gulf Coast of Florida. Many of these storms produced damaging wind gusts.  Also, one storm caused lightning damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hardee County 911 Center relayed a report of a snapped power pole in Zolfo Springs.  Time was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist air mass south of a stalled frontal boundary across the northern Gulf of Mexico provided instability for afternoon thunderstorms along the Gulf Coast of Florida. Many of these storms produced damaging wind gusts.  Also, one storm caused lightning damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Police Department building in Largo was struck by lightning, causing a minor fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist air mass south of a stalled frontal boundary across the northern Gulf of Mexico provided instability for afternoon thunderstorms along the Gulf Coast of Florida. Many of these storms produced damaging wind gusts.  Also, one storm caused lightning damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overhead traffic signals were blown down by thunderstorm wind gusts at Bryan Dairy Road and Starkey Road in Largo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist air mass south of a stalled frontal boundary setup a pattern of heavy rain and training storms, primarily on the 23rd and the 24th.  Some areas saw several inches of rain, resulting in street flooding and stalled vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and flooding was widely reported in the broadcast media around the Cape Coral area.  Cars were reported to be stalled in the water on Diplomat Parkway, and several roads were reported under water in Cape Coral and Bokeelia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist air mass south of a stalled frontal boundary setup a pattern of heavy rain and training storms, primarily on the 23rd and the 24th.  Some areas saw several inches of rain, resulting in street flooding and stalled vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Charlotte County Emergency Management reported considerable water across numerous roads and highways in Punta Gorda, and throughout the rest of Charlotte County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Easterly wind flow across the peninsula produces another active afternoon of severe weather across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station 1 mile east of Belleair, XBLA, recorded a wind gust of 69 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist air mass south of a stalled frontal boundary setup a pattern of heavy rain and training storms, primarily on the 23rd and the 24th.  Some areas saw several inches of rain, resulting in street flooding and stalled vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hernando County Emergency Management reported that heavy rain caused several road closures due to street flooding around Brooksville.  Additionally, Sparkman Lake overflowed its banks and flooded Campers Holiday RV Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak easterly winds and deep moisture allowed for sea breeze thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon. Some of these storms produced damaging lightning strikes and wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a report of wind damage to homes and an apartment building around Bay Street SE and 20th Avenue in Indian Rocks Beach.  A section of roof was blown off the apartment building on Bay Street SE and onto a road, and several trees and telephone poles were knocked down.  Additionally, property damage reported on 20th Avenue included a porch, a lanai, a roof, and a stop sign.  Time was estimated based off radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Andrea approached the Florida Big Bend from the southwest, producing heavy rain across east-central Florida over a 3-day period.  Area-wide totals of 2-4 inches were common, with isolated, maximum totals of 5-6 inches within Orange, Volusia and Osceola Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS station FL-VL-9 recorded a storm total of 5.49 of rain during Tropical Storm Andrea.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of major flash flooding occurred across parts of northwest Florida during the middle of August as an upper level trough developed west of the area with a continuous moisture feed off of the Gulf of Mexico streaming northward into the area.  This flooding hit many of the same areas impacted by the July flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Road 162 was flooded and impassible in spots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of deep tropical moisture combined with passing upper level disturbances sparks locally heavy rainfall producing cells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of rainfall was measured in Mandarin within the hour near the intersection of Old St. Augustine Road and Loretto Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep layer shear, steep mid-level lapse rates, and sufficient instability came together to produce several severe thunderstorms across the area.  Large hail was the primary threat, although some isolated occurrences of wind damage also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Florida Highway Patrol reported a tree down blocking Bloxham Cutoff.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough moved over Florida during the afternoon and evening which provided additional destabilization and moisture to support numerous strong to severe afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A JEA wind sensor measured at 62 mph wind gust (100 ft agl) at the Northside Power Station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A collision of three boundaries including the east coast sea breeze moving inland, and two outflow boundaries, one from the south and one from the west, converge under and intensifying thunderstorm over the Fort Caroline area. Rapid convergence and uplift spawned a brief tornado which caused structural damage to an apartment complex and widespread tree and some roof damage to homes in the area. One injury occurred. The slow storm motion also produced significant rainfall amounts, with caused flooding from Arlington toward the Mayport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in the Fort Caroline area, south of Monument Road. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved south into the region and stalled, a series of upper level disturbances moved through the area with broad southwest flow and moist conditions.  As the boundary became oriented parallel to the upper level winds, training bands of convection set up and produced some locally heavy rainfall, although flash flooding was limited to adjacent sections of southeastern Alabama.  A few strong to severe storms also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WJHG reported quarter sized hail in northern Gulf county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted northward over the Florida peninsula. During the afternoon, the front combined with an upper level short wave trough combined to trigger scattered severe storms over northeast Florida during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along 24th Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure retrograded from the Atlantic which focused afternoon thunderstorm activity over inland areas. Dry air aided a few strong storms to produce gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Camelot Chateau Assisted Living Facility was damaged when a tree when through the roof. The time was based on radar, and the cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front lifted northward over south Georgia with weaker SW steering flow in place. Diurnal heating combined with sea breeze and outflow activity spawned a few severe storms over the area during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters in Daytona North measured 5.7 to 6 inches of rainfall within 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of major flash flooding occurred across parts of northwest Florida during the middle of August as an upper level trough developed west of the area with a continuous moisture feed off of the Gulf of Mexico streaming northward into the area.  This flooding hit many of the same areas impacted by the July flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported over numerous south Walton county roads per the Walton Alert Facebook page.  Some roads were under more than a foot of water in developed areas near the coast with water washing up close to some homes on secondary roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper level disturbance rotating around a mean longwave trough west of the area brought copious amounts of moisture into the area.  The KTAE rawinsonde sounding on the evening of February 22nd measured a PWAT value of 1.77 inches, which was the 2nd highest PWAT observed during the month of February at that location.  Rainfall amounts generally between 9 and 14 inches were observed during the 5 day period between Feb 22-26 across the Tallahassee area with similar amounts across the remainder of the Florida Panhandle.  This led to areas of flooding.  There were also some severe storms during the early morning hours of Feb 26th.  The southeast big bend saw significantly less rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down along Hidden Lakes Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak easterly winds and deep moisture allowed for sea breeze thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon. Some of these storms produced damaging lightning strikes and wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Florida State Watch Office relayed a report that an apartment building on Belcher Road in Clearwater was struck by lightning, causing a fire. Time was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough of low pressure extending across South Florida from Tropical Depression Dorian produced scattered afternoon thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man was struck by lightning at the Miami Motorcross Park northwest of Hialeah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Easterly flow and deep moisture fueled numerous to scattered showers and thunderstorms. One of the storms produced a long lived funnel cloud that briefly touched down as a tornado in Lakewood Ranch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a picture of a very small tornado touching down on a lake in Lakewood Ranch.  Additionally, numerous funnel cloud pictures were submitted for this storms.  No damage was reported.  Exact times and locations were estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across the western Florida panhandle during the early evening hours producing sporadic damage.

The combination of multiple lingering convergent boundaries from previous convection, an approaching upper level short wave trough from the NW, and the sea breezes fueled scattered severe storms over an unstable airmass in the afternoon and evening. Gusty winds and heavy rainfall were the main hazards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to penny size hail was observed at the intersection of Blanding Blvd and Old Jennings Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of multiple lingering convergent boundaries from previous convection, an approaching upper level short wave trough from the NW, and the sea breezes fueled scattered severe storms over an unstable airmass in the afternoon and evening. Gusty winds and heavy rainfall were the main hazards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down over the roadway at County Road 136A and a power line was blown down by winds across County Road 129A. The time of damage based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper level disturbance rotating around a mean longwave trough west of the area brought copious amounts of moisture into the area.  The KTAE rawinsonde sounding on the evening of February 22nd measured a PWAT value of 1.77 inches, which was the 2nd highest PWAT observed during the month of February at that location.  Rainfall amounts generally between 9 and 14 inches were observed during the 5 day period between Feb 22-26 across the Tallahassee area with similar amounts across the remainder of the Florida Panhandle.  This led to areas of flooding.  There were also some severe storms during the early morning hours of Feb 26th.  The southeast big bend saw significantly less rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tobacco Road was washed out and closed.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous strong to severe thunderstorms developed within a very unstable airmass, aided by an upper level disturbance. Several of the storms produced penny to quarter sized hail and damaging winds across Lake, Orange and Seminole Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 3 to 4 inches in a short period of time, associated with a strong thunderstorm, caused a steep 30 foot section of ground adjacent to State Road 50 in Clermont to slide into a home. The mudslide destroyed a home on Sunnyside Drive. Mud several feet high entered the home. The drainage system in the area was designed several decades ago and could not handle the excessive rain rate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low moving across the eastern Gulf of Mexico provided increased instability for afternoon showers and thunderstorms.  Several of these storms caused damaging wind gusts.  Additionally, a couple lightning strikes caused fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported power lines down on Park Boulevard and on Bryan Dairy Road in Pinellas Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms occurred during July 21-23 with some storms becoming severe and knocking several trees down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Centerville Road and Cameron Chase Drive in Tallahassee blocking the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very anomalous pattern set up across the U.S. in early July featuring a persistent upper level trough over and just west of the Mississippi Valley and a downstream ridge off the Mid Atlantic Coast. This brought deep layer moist tropical air into the Southeast for several consecutive days resulting in torrential rains, flash flooding and river flooding. While several episodes of severe weather also occurred during this period, the heavy rain and accompanying flooding were the primary impacts.  Between 10 and 20 inches of rain fell in a three day period between July 2-4, and serious flooding occurred across portions of the Florida Panhandle with Walton, Holmes, and Washington counties declared federal disaster areas.  Damage estimates include $11 million for Walton county, $18.7 million for Washington County (including $3.7 million for the town of Vernon alone), $8.5 million for Holmes county, and $1 million for Bay county.  Most of the damage was due to roads and bridges, but some structures were also affected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve buildings were confirmed to have water entering them in or near Vernon.  Water was also approaching the shelter location, so the shelter had to be moved.  There were also still numerous road closures in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Easterly flow produced numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across the area. The storms produced sub severe hail and downed numerous trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hillsborough County 911 reported 2 trees down across a road north of Seffner. The event time was estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very anomalous pattern set up across the U.S. in early July featuring a persistent upper level trough over and just west of the Mississippi Valley and a downstream ridge off the Mid Atlantic Coast. This brought deep layer moist tropical air into the Southeast for several consecutive days resulting in torrential rains, flash flooding and river flooding. While several episodes of severe weather also occurred during this period, the heavy rain and accompanying flooding were the primary impacts.  Between 10 and 20 inches of rain fell in a three day period between July 2-4, and serious flooding occurred across portions of the Florida Panhandle with Walton, Holmes, and Washington counties declared federal disaster areas.  Damage estimates include $11 million for Walton county, $18.7 million for Washington County (including $3.7 million for the town of Vernon alone), $8.5 million for Holmes county, and $1 million for Bay county.  Most of the damage was due to roads and bridges, but some structures were also affected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was flowing over Church Street at an estimated depth of 8-10 inches just north of U.S. Highway 98.  Water was also reported over about a dozen other roads in Walton via the Walton Alert facebook page.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture and weak flow aloft allowed thunderstorms to develop around Tampa Bay on sea breeze and outflow boundaries. A couple waterspouts developed in these storms, one of which moved onshore as a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout over Pass-A-Grill Channel came onshore as a tornado.  The Pinellas County 911 Call Center reported power lines down on 44th Avenue.  Times and locations were estimated using radar data and 911 call logs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable airmass resulted in scattered afternoon showers and thunderstorms across South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pilot reported a funnel cloud 20 miles west of Palm Beach International Airport or about 9 miles SSW of Lion Country Safari.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable airmass resulted in scattered afternoon showers and thunderstorms across South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two funnel clouds were reported by Collier County Sheriffs Office near the Fiddlers Creek area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level low pressure trough off the southeast Florida coast produced an isolated late afternoon severe thunderstorm near Weston in Broward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report of an estimated 60 mph wind gust occurred in Weston and associated small trees down along with hail of unknown size. This was solicited and relayed through social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable atmosphere led to scattered afternoon thunderstorms across the interior of South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A home in West Boca was reported to be struck by lightning that damaged several windows, but nobody was injured. The strike occurred about 11 AM EDT in the 9600 block of Lancaster Place. The home's residents requested rescue crews check out the home. Crews found none of the electronics functioned and that several window frames had been separated from the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough of low pressure moved across South Florida producing scattered to numerous afternoon showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused a roof fire at a home in the 6000 block of Hudson Bay Lane in Lake Worth. Rescue crews arrived at the house and discovered a one-by-two foot hole in the roof of the home and quickly extinguished the fire. There were three people, four cats and one dog inside the home but there were no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large scale upper level trough of low pressure across the eastern U.S. along with a surface trough over the northwest Bahamas caused scattered showers and thunderstorms across South Florida during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Opa-Locka Airport recorded a wind gust of 52 Knots...60 mph...in an isolated severe thunderstorm. The Miami-Dade Fire Rescue at the airport also recorded a gust of 57 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough of low pressure moved across South Florida producing scattered to numerous afternoon showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning caused a roof fire to a home in Loxahatchee. Six people were in the house at the time, and Red Cross assistance was requested. Time based on archived lightning data courtesy of Earth Networks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The outer bands from Tropical Storm Andrea located along the South Carolina coast continued to move across southeast Florida resulting in torrential rains along the coastal sections of Broward and northeast Miami-Dade Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources including the broadcast media, local storm spotters and city officials reported severe flooding from a nearly stationary thunderstorm across the northeast portion of Miami-Dade County extending northward into the Hollywood area of southeast Broward County. The first report was from the media of impassable roads in the Waterways section of Aventura, north of Aventura Mall at 4:53 PM EDT with reports several minutes later from the media relayed from the Aventura Police Department that over 50 vehicles were disabled in that same area. A trained spotter in Hollywood then reported that as of 5:17 PM EDT, they had received 9 inches of rain and that vehicles were disabled near the intersection of Sheridan St. and A1A, with flooding occurring in retention ponds and nearby roads. Portions of Hallandale Beach, especially near the Intracoastal Waterway were severely flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms developed over the area under the influence of the sea breezes and outflow boundaries fueled by a moist and unstable airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An oak tree was blown down in a parking lot near the intersection of Bowden Road and Parental Home Road, which damaged a car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms developed over the area under the influence of the sea breezes and outflow boundaries fueled by a moist and unstable airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were blown down throughout the city of Gainesville. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms developed over the area under the influence of the sea breezes and outflow boundaries fueled by a moist and unstable airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down that damaged a roof. Other trees and power lines were blown down in the Ocala area. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level impulse tracked southward over north Florida. This feature combined with the west coast sea breeze and outflows interacted over a moist and unstable airmass which spawned scattered severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to nickel size hail was reported at Fort Matanzas National Monument.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep and moist SW flow with 20-25 knots of 0-6km steering flow on the east side of a deepening upper level trough brought widespread thunderstorm activity across the Suwannee River Valley of northeast Florida by midday where isolated wet downburst activity caused some tree and power line damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were blown down near the Columbia and Baker county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning caused damage to homes in Flagler Beach and near Palm Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck two homes in the Plantation Bay subdivision. The cost of electrical damage was estimated for inclusion of the event in Storm Data. The estimated is likely an underestimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning caused damage to homes in Flagler Beach and near Palm Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home in the Grand Haven subdivision which caused damage to the roof and soffits. The cost of damage was estimated for the event to be included in Storm Data. This estimate is likely an underestimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lone severe storm produced a dowmburst that impacted a property off of County Road 51 near Luraville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A residence off of County Road 51 in Luraville suffered downburst wind damage which included downed trees, roof damage and porch damage. The cost of damage was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of passing upper level short wave energy...lingering low level outflow boundaries from prior convective activity...and the sea breezes spawned scattered severe storms during the afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported trees down over Ogilvie Road and the intersection of River Road and Wrights Dairy Road near Callahan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of passing upper level short wave energy...lingering low level outflow boundaries from prior convective activity...and the sea breezes spawned scattered severe storms during the afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple tall trees were uprooted and a shed was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms developed over the area under the influence of the sea breezes and outflow boundaries fueled by a moist and unstable airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was observed at San Jose Blvd and Interstate 295 and at I-295 and Old St. Augustine Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms developed over the area under the influence of the sea breezes and outflow boundaries fueled by a moist and unstable airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms developed over the area under the influence of the sea breezes and outflow boundaries fueled by a moist and unstable airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter measured 60 mph wind gusts at Old St. Augustine Road and Loretto Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ample low level moisture and a ridge axis across Florida setup a late afternoon sea breeze collision over southwest Florida late in the afternoon.  Some of the storms produced small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A broadcast media member's social media page had a report of penny sized hail in southwest Cape Coral.  Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ample low level moisture and a ridge axis across Florida setup a late afternoon sea breeze collision over southwest Florida late in the afternoon.  Some of the storms produced small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SKYWARN spotter SAR-179 reported nickle sized hail 8 miles east of interstate 75 on Fruitville Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough was stretched out from the offshore coastal waters to the Suwannee River valley, while the surface cold front was shifting southward over south Georgia. These features combined with high shear and surface based instability fueled scattered severe storms during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was reported along Harlow Blvd. Anvers Blvd, Daughtery Blvd, and Cinderella Road all had street flooding. All drainage ditches in the area were near overflow. Storm total rainfall was 2.64 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough was stretched out from the offshore coastal waters to the Suwannee River valley, while the surface cold front was shifting southward over south Georgia. These features combined with high shear and surface based instability fueled scattered severe storms during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and several large limbs were blown down along County Road 227 in Sampson. Minor flooding was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough was stretched out from the offshore coastal waters to the Suwannee River valley, while the surface cold front was shifting southward over south Georgia. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was reported in the Villages of Mayport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface stalled over NE Florida. This feature combined with a moist and unstable airmass coincided with sea breezes which triggered strong afternoon thunderstorms over NE Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size was observed in Orange Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Andrea crossed the forecast area from the Gulf of Mexico. SE Ga and NE Fl were on the northeast side of the storm...which focused onshore convergent bands. A few tornadoes were spawned along the Duval and Nassau county Atlantic coasts...with gusty tropical storm force winds and locally heavy rainfall over inland areas. Twenty-four hour rainfall amounts ranged from 1 to 4 inches...with the highest value of 3.62 inches 4 ESE of Waycross. Rainfall amounts decreased to the SE with an observation site 5 NNW of Spuds only reporting 0.06 inches. Wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph were common along the coast and estuary systems. The highest gust over northeast Fl was 38 knots measured at Naval Air Station Jacksonville from the south on the 6th at 11:06 pm. The highest gust over southeast Georgia was 39 knots measured from the WNW at 6:28 am at the St. Simons Airport. The highest wind gust along the coast was reported from a WeatherFlow (mesonet) station located at the Jacksonville Beach Pier with a speed of 72 knots reported at 4:37 pm on the 6th. This gust was likely caused by a passing waterspout that moved onshore near Mayport. The lowest pressure measured was from the Jekyll Island WeatherFlow (mesonet) station of 993.0 mb on the 7th at 12:30 am. The highest wind gust measured over the adjacent Atlantic waters was 39 kts at buoy 41012 43 NM east of St. Augustine on the 6th at 10:50 pm. The C-Man station SAUF1 in St. Augustine measured a gust to 42 knots on the 6th at 7:58 pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS survey determined an EF1 tornado with winds of 75 to 90 mph impacted the extreme eastern side of Mayport Naval Station. Several non-critical structures were impacted with minor damage. The length of the tornado across the base was .32 miles however it is likely the funnel continued into the river as a waterspout. The maximum width just before it entered the river was 0.1 miles (176 yards). Damage was generally confined to roof damage and broken windows. At 5:12 pm, there was a report of a tornado on the ground in Fernandina Beach near the 1300 block of North Fletcher Ave. The NWS survey revealed that EF1 intensity of 100 mph winds impacted Amelia Island. The storm cross the beach along A1A south of Rachael Ave and progressed north-northwest near and east of Egans Creek. The tornado passed through the Starboard Landing neighborhood with 80 to 100 mph winds and ended near Sunrise Drive. The path was approximately 2.25 miles and the maximum width was 0.4 miles or about 2,050 feet near Jasmine Street.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and large limbs were knocked down at Fort Matanzas National Monument. Winds were estimated at 60 to 70 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were blown down in Arlington.

These features combined with high shear and surface based instability fueled scattered severe storms during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down on homes along Hidden Waters Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture combined with a weakening cold front front approaching southwest Georgia in concert with the east coast sea breeze and outflow boundaries...sparked scattered strong and rotating storms over the area during the afternoon and evening near the east coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were estimated at 60 mph in the Timber Creek subdivision, just west of Interstate 95.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front lingered over south Georgia while a moist tropical airmass streamed over the area under southwest flow. Locally heavy rainfall was expected due to slow storm motion...and a few strong storms were expected during the afternoon due to outflows from morning convection over western zones interacting with an unstable low level airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was reported in Arlington on Free Avenue, Galveston Ave, and Dandee Ave. Lawn debris was flowing out of yards and into the streets. The water was ankle deep in some areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prevailing SW flow focused a sea breeze collision near the St. Johns River basin during the afternoon where a lone severe storms produced some wind damage in Clay county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was broken at the top and downed power lines which caused power outages in the area. The time of the event was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough was stretched out from the offshore coastal waters to the Suwannee River valley, while the surface cold front was shifting southward over south Georgia. These features combined with high shear and surface based instability fueled scattered severe storms during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree and fence were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Active SW steering flow and sea breezes over a moist and unstable airmass triggered pulse severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down on County Road 235 and NW 22nd Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest steering flow over a moist and unstable airmass combined with cool mid level temps of -9 to -10 deg C at 500 mb fueled a few strong storms during the afternoon which initiated along the sea breezes and outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds blew down trees and power lines, which injured one person SSE of Macclenny.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest steering flow over a moist and unstable airmass combined with cool mid level temps of -9 to -10 deg C at 500 mb fueled a few strong storms during the afternoon which initiated along the sea breezes and outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs and trees were blown down in Fort Caroline. The time of the event was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough was stretched out from the offshore coastal waters to the Suwannee River valley, while the surface cold front was shifting southward over south Georgia. These features combined with high shear and surface based instability fueled scattered severe storms during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to penny size hail was reported on the Buckman Bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Andrea moving across the northeast Gulf of Mexico produced outer rain bands that moved through South Florida resulting in scattered severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado tracked almost due north in the Acreage community of north central Palm Beach County. The tornado moved across a suburban, residential community just west of 130th Path North between 69th St. North and 87th St. North. Several homes sustained minor to moderate roof damage, mainly in the form of shingles and roof covering torn off. A couple of homes sustained more substantial rood damage with partial uplift of roofs. Numerous trees were uprooted or sheared off near the trunk, along with plenty of large branches broken or split from the main trunk. Trees damaged consisted primarily of Oaks, Slash Pines and Palms. Flying debris from trees resulted in broken windows in several homes.||One serious injury required hospitalization in a home on 76th Road North. An 85-year old woman was injured when she was struck by flying debris from a large Oak tree which broke through her bedroom window. One home sustained damage to the garage door, causing the door to blow in and leading to the roof being completely punctured above the garage. A few vehicles were moved from their original locations and a 30-foot boat was flipped on its side.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Andrea developed in the Gulf of Mexico on the morning of the 5th and moved northeast, making landfall 10 miles south of Steinhatchee, FL early on the evening of the 6th.  At it's peak, Tropical Storm Andrea had a minimum surface pressure of 992 MB.  ||Tropical storm force winds were estimated by the National Hurricane Center Hurricane Research Division to extend along the coast as far south as Pinellas County, including extending several miles inland in Levy County.  Additionally, several weather stations in Hillsborough County along the Tampa Bay measured frequent gusts and even sustained winds of 34 knots or greater.  Sites farther south measuring only occasional wind gusts inside of thunderstorms.  The highest sustained wind recorded was 41 knots at the ARPSWXNET mesonet site CW6730 on Davis Island in Hillsborough Bay.  The highest wind gust recorded was 49 knots also at CW6730.||Rainfall totals across the area were as high as 6 inches for the 5th-7th, with the highest values over Nature Coast through Tampa Bay region.  The highest single station storm total rainfall was at the CoCoRaHS site FL-LV-9 located 8.5 miles east northeast of Chiefland in Levy County, measuring the 6.17 inches.  Minor street flooding was reported in Levy, Citrus, Sumter, Hernando, Pasco, Pinellas, Hillsborough, Manatee, Sarasota, De Soto, and Charlotte Counties. ||Storm surge was relatively minor, with sites from Tampa Bay to Cedar Key recording storm tides of up to 6 feet MLLW, and storm surge of 2-4 feet. South of Tampa Bay, storm surge and storm tide were both less than 3 feet.  The highest storm tide recorded was 6.26 feet MLLW at the tide gauge at Cedar Key, with a storm surge of 4.08 feet MLLW calculated by subtracting the predicted tide from the storm tide.||In Levy County, tropical storm force winds were felt along and just inland of the coast during the afternoon of the 6th.  The highest gust recorded was 44 knots at both the CKYF1 and CDRF1 weather stations in Cedar Key, with both sites also recording periods of sustained winds of 35 knots. Storm total precipitation ranged from 2 inches to just over 6 inches across the county, with the CoCoRaHS site FL-LV-9 located 8.5 miles east northeast of Chiefland measuring the highest total of 6.17 inches. Peak storm tide at the Cedar Key tide gauge reached 6.26 feet MLLW on the afternoon of the 6th. Subtracting the predicted astronomical tide, the calculated highest storm surge was 4.08 feet MLLW late in the afternoon of the 6th.||In Citrus County, tropical storm force winds were felt along the coast during the afternoon of the 6th.  Storm total precipitation ranged from 2 inches to almost 6 inches across the county, with the CoCoRaHS site FL-CT-7 located 5.3 miles north-northeast of Crystal River measuring the highest total of 5.87 inches. Peak storm tide was estimated to be around 5 feet MLLW on the afternoon of the 6th. Subtracting the predicted astronomical tide, the highest storm surge was estimated to be around 3 to 4 feet MLLW late in the afternoon of the 6th.||In Hernando County, tropical storm force winds were felt along the coast during the afternoon of the 6th. Storm total precipitation ranged from around 2.5 inches to around 4.5 inches across the county, with the CoCoRaHS site FL-HN-8 located 3 miles north of Weeki Wachee measuring the highest total of 4.69 inches. Peak storm tide was estimated to be around 4 to 5 feet MLLW on the afternoon of the 6th. Subtracting the predicted astronomical tide, the highest storm surge was estimated to be around 3 to 4 feet MLLW late in the afternoon of the 6th.||In Pasco County, tropical storm force winds were felt along the coast during the afternoon of the 6th. Storm total precipitation across the county ranged from 2 inches to just over 6 inches, with the CoCoRaHS site FL-PS-43 located 1.5 miles south-southeast of Land O' Lakes measuring the highest total of 6.07 inches. Peak storm tide was estimated to be around 4 feet MLLW on the afternoon of the 6th. Subtracting the predicted astronomical tide, the highest storm surge was estimated to be around 3 feet MLLW late in the afternoon of the 6th.||In Pinellas County, tropical storm force winds were felt along the coast during the afternoon of the 6th. The highest gust recorded was 43 knots at both the NOW-NWLON station CWBF1 in Clearwater and the APRSWXNET site CW4299 on Treasure Island. Additionally, the CWBF1 weather station in Clearwater Beach measured sustained winds of 35 knots. Storm total precipitation ranged from 2.5 inches to over 5 inches across the county, with the CoCoRaHS site FL-PN-17 located 0.6 miles east of South Pasadena measuring the highest total of 5.37 inches. Peak storm tide at the Clearwater tide gauge on the Gulf Coast measured 4.49 feet MLLW on the afternoon of the 6th. At the Saint Petersburg tide gauge in Tampa Bay, the storm tide was 4.28 feet. Subtracting the predicted astronomical tide, the calculated highest storm surges for Clearwater and Saint Petersburg, respectively, were 2.92 and 2.47 feet MLLW late in the afternoon of the 6th.||In Hillsborough County, tropical storm force winds were felt along the coast during the afternoon of the 6th. The highest gust recorded was 49 knots at the ARPSWXNET mesonet site CW6730 on Davis Island in Hillsborough Bay. Additionally, the same site measured sustained winds of 41 knots. Storm total precipitation across the county ranged from 2 inches to over 5 inches, with the CoCoRaHS site FL-HB-37 located 2 miles east of Lutz measuring the highest total of 5.21 inches. Peak storm tide at the Old Port Tampa tide gauge measured 4.82 feet MLLW on the afternoon of the 6th. At the McKay Bay Entrance tide gauge, the storm tide was 5.05 feet. Subtracting the predicted astronomical tide, the calculated highest storm surges for Old Port Tampa and Mckay Bay Entrance, respectively, were 2.80 and 2.97 feet MLLW late in the afternoon of the 6th.

The highest gust recorded was 49 knots at the ARPSWXNET mesonet site CW6730 on Davis Island in Hillsborough Bay. Additionally, the same site measured sustained winds of 41 knots. Storm total precipitation across the county ranged from 2 inches to over 5 inches, with the CoCoRaHS site FL-HB-37 located 2 miles east of Lutz measuring the highest total of 5.21 inches. Peak storm tide at the Old Port Tampa tide gauge measured 4.82 feet MLLW on the afternoon of the 6th. At the McKay Bay Entrance tide gauge, the storm tide was 5.05 feet. Subtracting the predicted astronomical tide, the calculated highest storm surges for Old Port Tampa and Mckay Bay Entrance, respectively, were 2.80 and 2.97 feet MLLW late in the afternoon of the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service survey found a narrow path of damage cut through the heart of Sun City Center several blocks west of State Highway 301.  The damage path was south to north and included downed trees, damage to lanais and facia, and minor shingle damage. The worst damage was near Cherry Hill Drive and North Pebble Beach Blvd, where shingles were stripped off homes. Hillsborough County Department of Emergency Management estimated the damage at $36,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable atmosphere led to scattered afternoon thunderstorms across the interior of South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 57 year old male died and his 56 year old friend was injured when their boat was struck by lightning on Lake Okeechobee. The two men were boating and decided to dock at the Clewiston boat ramps as a thunderstorm approached. As they were making their way back to Clewiston, lightning struck their boat and both men were knocked unconscious. Some time later, the 56 year old male awoke and called 911 after realizing what happened. Hendry County EMS arrived on the scene and transported the unconscious male to Hendry County Regional Medical Center where he was pronounced dead. The other male was later taken to Palms West Hospital to be treated for his injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical wave moved across Cuba enhancing scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms over South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning fatally struck a 35 year old male construction worker who was working on the exterior of a single family home on scaffolding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon on sea breeze and outflow boundaries and continued through the early evening.  A lightning strike from one of these storms damaged a boat in Central Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire department responded to a report of a lightning strike to a boat sitting on a trailer on Rome Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow fed sea breeze storms moving into the Tampa Bay area late in the afternoon.  One of these storms produced a waterspout that came onshore as a tornado.  Additionally, a couple lightning strikes caused fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pinellas County 911 call center reported minor damage to a shed caused by a lightning strike on Belcher Road in Largo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Andrea moving across the northeast Gulf of Mexico produced outer rain bands that moved through South Florida resulting in scattered severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported a funnel cloud near the intersection of Southern Blvd. and State Road 7. The funnel did not touch down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Andrea moving across the northeast Gulf of Mexico produced outer rain bands that moved through South Florida resulting in scattered severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm chasers reported a tornado touching down just east of U.S. 27 about 6 miles north of Alligator Alley/I-75 in Broward County. The storm producing the tornado moved almost due north and the tornado likely crossed into Palm Beach County east of US 27 and southwest of Loxahatchee National Wildlife Refuge before lifting back up and dissipating. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Andrea moving across the northeast Gulf of Mexico produced outer rain bands that moved through South Florida resulting in scattered severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm chasers reported a tornado touching down just east of U.S. 27 about 6 miles north of Alligator Alley/I-75. The tornado likely crossed into Palm Beach County before lifting back up and dissipating. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow fed sea breeze storms moving into the Tampa Bay area late in the afternoon.  One of these storms produced a waterspout that came onshore as a tornado.  Additionally, a couple lightning strikes caused fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pinellas County 911 call center reported a small fire in a house on Pine Place in Clearwater caused by a lightning strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The outer bands from Tropical Storm Andrea located along the South Carolina coast continued to move across southeast Florida resulting in torrential rains along the coastal sections of Broward and northeast Miami-Dade Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources including the broadcast media, local storm spotters and city officials reported severe flooding from a nearly stationary thunderstorm across the northeast portion of Miami-Dade County extending northward into the Hollywood area of southeast Broward County. The first report was from the media of impassable roads in the Waterways section of Aventura, north of Aventura Mall at 4:53 PM EDT with reports several minutes later from the media relayed from the Aventura Police Department that over 50 vehicles were disabled in that same area. A trained spotter at the same time reported disabled vehicles and impassable roads near NE 170th St. and NE 15th Ave. in North Miami. Several reports of flooding were received between 5:30 PM and 9 PM EDT across the same areas. Severe flooding was reported in the 1200 block of NE 144 Street which required the relocation of 24 families. City officials in Golden Beach of northeast Miami-Dade County reported hundreds of people stranded in their vehicles. An off-duty NWS employee also reported significant flooding in his condominium parking garage in Aventura. A South Florida Water Management District rain gauge recorded 13.15 inches of rain in North Miami Beach at 5:53 PM EDT with storm total at the same gauge by 9 PM EDT recording 13.94 inches. Other rainfall reports received were 11.71 inches at the FIU Biscayne Campus in North Miami Beach and 9.89 inches at North Miami/Keystone Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow fed sea breeze storms moving into the Tampa Bay area late in the afternoon.  One of these storms produced a waterspout that came onshore as a tornado.  Additionally, a couple lightning strikes caused fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout that developed over the Tampa Bay near the west end of the Gandy Bridge came onshore as a tornado near the Weedon Island Power Plant.  The 911 Call Center reported multiple trees down, a dock pushed against a house, and a lanai damaged on 87th Avenue in Saint Petersburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow led to early afternoon sea breeze thunderstorms over the Nature Coast.  A lightning strike from one of the storms hit a house and sparked a house fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported Fire Rescue crews reported to the scene of a house fire in Floral City, FL, believed to be caused by a lightning strike.  No one was home at the time and no injuries were reported.  Damage was estimated to be around $95,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep layer of moisture and weak flow allowed for sea breeze thunderstorms to form and move inland through the afternoon.  One storm produced 3/4 inch hail in Lakeland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Twitter report of nickel sized hail in Lakeland was received around 7:30 PM EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Andrea developed in the Gulf of Mexico on the morning of the 5th and moved northeast, making landfall 10 miles south of Steinhatchee, FL early on the evening of the 6th.  At it's peak, Tropical Storm Andrea had a minimum surface pressure of 992 MB.  ||Tropical storm force winds were estimated by the National Hurricane Center Hurricane Research Division to extend along the coast as far south as Pinellas County, including extending several miles inland in Levy County.  Additionally, several weather stations in Hillsborough County along the Tampa Bay measured frequent gusts and even sustained winds of 34 knots or greater.  Sites farther south measuring only occasional wind gusts inside of thunderstorms.  The highest sustained wind recorded was 41 knots at the ARPSWXNET mesonet site CW6730 on Davis Island in Hillsborough Bay.  The highest wind gust recorded was 49 knots also at CW6730.||Rainfall totals across the area were as high as 6 inches for the 5th-7th, with the highest values over Nature Coast through Tampa Bay region.  The highest single station storm total rainfall was at the CoCoRaHS site FL-LV-9 located 8.5 miles east northeast of Chiefland in Levy County, measuring the 6.17 inches.  Minor street flooding was reported in Levy, Citrus, Sumter, Hernando, Pasco, Pinellas, Hillsborough, Manatee, Sarasota, De Soto, and Charlotte Counties. ||Storm surge was relatively minor, with sites from Tampa Bay to Cedar Key recording storm tides of up to 6 feet MLLW, and storm surge of 2-4 feet. South of Tampa Bay, storm surge and storm tide were both less than 3 feet.  The highest storm tide recorded was 6.26 feet MLLW at the tide gauge at Cedar Key, with a storm surge of 4.08 feet MLLW calculated by subtracting the predicted tide from the storm tide.||In Levy County, tropical storm force winds were felt along and just inland of the coast during the afternoon of the 6th.  The highest gust recorded was 44 knots at both the CKYF1 and CDRF1 weather stations in Cedar Key, with both sites also recording periods of sustained winds of 35 knots. Storm total precipitation ranged from 2 inches to just over 6 inches across the county, with the CoCoRaHS site FL-LV-9 located 8.5 miles east northeast of Chiefland measuring the highest total of 6.17 inches. Peak storm tide at the Cedar Key tide gauge reached 6.26 feet MLLW on the afternoon of the 6th. Subtracting the predicted astronomical tide, the calculated highest storm surge was 4.08 feet MLLW late in the afternoon of the 6th.||In Citrus County, tropical storm force winds were felt along the coast during the afternoon of the 6th.  Storm total precipitation ranged from 2 inches to almost 6 inches across the county, with the CoCoRaHS site FL-CT-7 located 5.3 miles north-northeast of Crystal River measuring the highest total of 5.87 inches. Peak storm tide was estimated to be around 5 feet MLLW on the afternoon of the 6th. Subtracting the predicted astronomical tide, the highest storm surge was estimated to be around 3 to 4 feet MLLW late in the afternoon of the 6th.||In Hernando County, tropical storm force winds were felt along the coast during the afternoon of the 6th. Storm total precipitation ranged from around 2.5 inches to around 4.5 inches across the county, with the CoCoRaHS site FL-HN-8 located 3 miles north of Weeki Wachee measuring the highest total of 4.69 inches. Peak storm tide was estimated to be around 4 to 5 feet MLLW on the afternoon of the 6th. Subtracting the predicted astronomical tide, the highest storm surge was estimated to be around 3 to 4 feet MLLW late in the afternoon of the 6th.||In Pasco County, tropical storm force winds were felt along the coast during the afternoon of the 6th. Storm total precipitation across the county ranged from 2 inches to just over 6 inches, with the CoCoRaHS site FL-PS-43 located 1.5 miles south-southeast of Land O' Lakes measuring the highest total of 6.07 inches. Peak storm tide was estimated to be around 4 feet MLLW on the afternoon of the 6th. Subtracting the predicted astronomical tide, the highest storm surge was estimated to be around 3 feet MLLW late in the afternoon of the 6th.||In Pinellas County, tropical storm force winds were felt along the coast during the afternoon of the 6th. The highest gust recorded was 43 knots at both the NOW-NWLON station CWBF1 in Clearwater and the APRSWXNET site CW4299 on Treasure Island. Additionally, the CWBF1 weather station in Clearwater Beach measured sustained winds of 35 knots. Storm total precipitation ranged from 2.5 inches to over 5 inches across the county, with the CoCoRaHS site FL-PN-17 located 0.6 miles east of South Pasadena measuring the highest total of 5.37 inches. Peak storm tide at the Clearwater tide gauge on the Gulf Coast measured 4.49 feet MLLW on the afternoon of the 6th. At the Saint Petersburg tide gauge in Tampa Bay, the storm tide was 4.28 feet. Subtracting the predicted astronomical tide, the calculated highest storm surges for Clearwater and Saint Petersburg, respectively, were 2.92 and 2.47 feet MLLW late in the afternoon of the 6th.||In Hillsborough County, tropical storm force winds were felt along the coast during the afternoon of the 6th. The highest gust recorded was 49 knots at the ARPSWXNET mesonet site CW6730 on Davis Island in Hillsborough Bay. Additionally, the same site measured sustained winds of 41 knots. Storm total precipitation across the county ranged from 2 inches to over 5 inches, with the CoCoRaHS site FL-HB-37 located 2 miles east of Lutz measuring the highest total of 5.21 inches. Peak storm tide at the Old Port Tampa tide gauge measured 4.82 feet MLLW on the afternoon of the 6th. At the McKay Bay Entrance tide gauge, the storm tide was 5.05 feet. Subtracting the predicted astronomical tide, the calculated highest storm surges for Old Port Tampa and Mckay Bay Entrance, respectively, were 2.80 and 2.97 feet MLLW late in the afternoon of the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tide gauge at Clearwater measured a peak tide of 4.49 feet MLLW on the afternoon of the 6th.  Subtracting the predicted astronomical tide, the calculated highest storm surge was 2.92 feet late in the afternoon of the 6th. The tide gauge at Saint Petersburg measured a peak tide of 4.28 feet MLLW on the afternoon of the 6th.  Subtracting the predicted astronomical tide, the calculated highest storm surge was 2.47 feet late in the afternoon of the 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level trough over the southeastern U.S. strengthened sea breeze thunderstorms over West Central Florida.  Some of these storms produced nickel sized hail in Polk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a report from the public of nickel sized hail between Fort Meade and Frostproof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak trough passing through northern Florida intensified afternoon sea breeze thunderstorms.  One of these storms produced wind gusts estimated at 40 to 50 MPH that damaged and awning an knocked power lines down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage was reported by the public to an awning and powerlines. Wind gusts were estimated to be 40 to 50 MPH with the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late afternoon sea breeze boundary collision over the Nature Coast allowed thunderstorms to rapidly intensify.  One of these storms produced a downburst with estimated winds of up to 60 MPH, damaging several homes and knocking down power lines and trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hernando County Sheriff's Office reported approximately 30 calls from residents in eastern Hernando County around Mondon Hill Road, Weatherly Road, and Croom Road from a storm that passed by around 5:30 PM EDT.  Damage reported included numerous power poles down, trees down, and minor roof damage to homes, a barn, and sheds.  Time is estimated from radar and 911 call logs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable atmosphere along with a weak cold front in Central Florida resulted in scattered late afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Funnel clouds were reported by Hendry County Sheriff's Department near the Lee County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Andrea moving across the northeast Gulf of Mexico produced outer rain bands that moved through South Florida resulting in scattered severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado moved across Belle Glade with reports of trees down and damage to an awning near 1000 S. Main St. and several power lines down near 400 Martin Luther King Blvd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rip current fatality occurred at Huguenot Memorial Park on Fort George Island which was an unguarded beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A male was killed from a rip current. The time of the incident was unknown and entered at 16:00 EDT for inclusion of the event in StormData.
